 {"id":"wa-bsf9","title":"Extension sandboxing: WASM or Lua runtime for safe custom code execution","description":"
# Extension Sandboxing

## Purpose
Run custom extension code safely without risking system security.

## Options Evaluated

### 1. WASM (Recommended)
- Pros: Strong isolation, multi-language, proven
- Cons: Larger binary, more complex
- Libraries: wasmtime, wasmer

### 2. Lua
- Pros: Simple, fast, battle-tested (Neovim/WezTerm)
- Cons: Single language, needs careful sandboxing
- Libraries: rlua, mlua

## Recommendation: WASM via wasmtime
- Better isolation guarantees
- Supports Rust, Go, C, AssemblyScript
- WASI for controlled system access

## Sandbox Capabilities
```rust
pub struct ExtensionSandbox {
    engine: wasmtime::Engine,
    linker: wasmtime::Linker<SandboxState>,
    capabilities: SandboxCapabilities,
}

pub struct SandboxCapabilities {
    pub read_pane_output: bool,
    pub send_notifications: bool,
    pub http_requests: bool,
    pub file_access: FileAccessScope,
}
```

## Capability Levels
- Level 1: Read-only (patterns only)
- Level 2: Read + notify (most workflows)
- Level 3: Read + notify + HTTP (integrations)
- Level 4: Full (admin extensions)

## Extension Manifest
```toml
[extension]
name = "my-patterns"
capabilities = ["read_pane_output"]
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] WASM runtime integrated
- [ ] Capability-based permissions
- [ ] Extensions cannot access system without permission
- [ ] Untrusted extension cannot crash wa

## Testing
- Unit tests for sandbox policy enforcement and resource limits.
- Integration tests running a restricted extension in a sandbox.
- Security checks: verify no filesystem/network escape.

 {"id":"wa-bv81","title":"E2E script: workspace isolation (no cross-project DB leakage; --workspace honored)","description":"# Task: E2E script — workspace isolation

## Goal
Prove that wa honors workspace scoping and does not leak data across projects.

This is a critical UX property:
- users run multiple projects in parallel
- agents should not read/write the wrong DB
- debugging depends on knowing which workspace you are looking at

## Scenario
- Spawn a dummy pane that prints a unique token: WORKSPACE_TOKEN.

Steps
1) Start wa watch with --workspace <A>.
2) Wait until WORKSPACE_TOKEN is persisted (wa robot search --workspace <A>).
3) Run wa robot search --workspace <B> for WORKSPACE_TOKEN.

Assertions
- In workspace A: the token is found.
- In workspace B: the token is NOT found.

Optional extension
- Start wa watch in workspace B and ensure it creates a distinct DB.

## Artifacts
- effective config for both workspaces
- wa watch logs
- wa robot search outputs for A and B

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test is deterministic.
- Failures make it obvious whether the bug is:
  - workspace resolution
  - DB path derivation
  - CLI flag propagation


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Assert the two workspaces produce distinct DB/log paths (not just search behavior).
  - Add a control assertion that workspace B can find a different token once it has ingested something.

- Artifact validation:
  - Artifacts must include the derived paths (workspace root, db path, log path) to make mis-scoping diagnosable.

 {"id":"wa-c5dn","title":"Implement write batching + WAL checkpoint/optimize strategy","description":"# Task: Write batching + checkpoint/optimize strategy

## Goal
Reduce write amplification and keep WAL/FTS healthy under sustained ingest.

## Requirements
- Writer batching:
  - batch segment inserts and index updates
  - bounded batch size (time/rows/bytes)
- WAL strategy:
  - periodic checkpoints based on size/time
  - avoid long stalls (do work incrementally)
- FTS maintenance:
  - decide when to run optimize/vacuum-like operations
  - ensure maintenance is explicit and does not surprise users

## Testing
- Integration tests:
  - simulate sustained ingest and assert:
    - WAL does not grow without bound
    - queries remain available
- Benchmarks:
  - compare before/after ingest throughput

## Acceptance Criteria
- Sustained ingest remains stable and storage maintenance is predictable.

 {"id":"wa-c7o","title":"[EPIC] Phase 2: Workflow Engine & Automation","description":"# Phase 2: Workflow Engine & Automation

## Overview
This epic builds the workflow automation system that responds to detected patterns. The workflow engine is what makes wa "active" rather than just a passive monitor - it can take corrective actions when agents hit limits, experience compaction, need authentication, etc.

## Strategic Importance
The workflow system transforms wa from a "monitoring tool" into an "automation platform". This is where the real value emerges:
- Automatic context recovery after compaction
- Automatic account rotation on usage limits
- Automatic authentication renewal
- Foundation for more sophisticated multi-agent coordination

## Key Components

### 1. Workflow Engine Architecture
- Trait-based workflow definition: \`Workflow\` trait with name, description, handles, steps, execute_step
- Step results: Continue, Done, Retry, Abort, WaitFor
- WaitCondition: Pattern, PaneIdle, External
- Per-pane workflow locking (prevent conflicting workflows)
- Idempotent, recoverable, audited execution

### 2. Handle Usage Limits Workflow (Codex First)
Steps:
1. Exit current session gracefully (Ctrl-C twice for Codex)
2. Parse session info for resume (session ID, token usage)
3. Select next available account
4. Perform account authentication
5. Resume previous session
6. Send continue prompt

This is the most complex workflow and proves out the architecture.

### 3. Handle Compaction Workflow
Steps:
1. Wait for compaction to complete (stabilization window)
2. Send agent-specific context refresh prompt
   - Claude Code: "Reread AGENTS.md so it's still fresh in your mind."
   - Codex: "Please re-read AGENTS.md and any key project context files."
   - Gemini: "Please re-examine AGENTS.md and project context."

### 4. Browser Automation Skeleton
- Playwright integration structure
- Browser profile persistence
- OpenAI device auth flow (skeleton)
- Anthropic OAuth flow (skeleton)
- Google auth flow (skeleton)
- Semi-automated fallback (open browser for human when automation fails)

### 5. CAUT Integration
- Shell out to \`caut\` for usage tracking
- get_usage(service) - current usage for OpenAI/Anthropic/Google
- refresh_usage(service) - refresh from APIs
- Account selection logic based on percent_remaining

## Workflow Design Constraints
Every workflow must be:
- **Idempotent**: Re-running doesn't make a mess
- **Recoverable**: Can resume after a crash
- **Audited**: Every step recorded (what was observed, what was sent)
- **Guarded**: Each step validates state before action

## Workflow Execution Model
Each step follows: Observe → Decide → Act → Verify

Step can return:
- \`Continue\` - proceed to next step
- \`WaitFor(condition)\` - pause until condition met
- \`Retry(after)\` - retry same step after delay
- \`Abort(reason)\` - stop workflow with error
- \`Done(result)\` - workflow completed successfully

## Data-Driven Workflow Descriptors
In addition to compiled Rust workflows, support YAML/TOML descriptors for simple prompt injection sequences:

\`\`\`yaml
# ~/.config/wa/workflows/custom_compaction.yaml
name: custom_compaction_prompt
trigger:
  event: session.compaction
  agent: claude_code
steps:
  - wait_stable: 2s
  - send: |
      Please re-read AGENTS.md and the project's README.md.
  - wait_for: "❯"
\`\`\`

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Workflow engine can execute multi-step workflows
- [ ] handle_compaction workflow works end-to-end for Claude Code
- [ ] handle_usage_limits workflow works end-to-end for Codex (at least skeleton)
- [ ] Browser automation structure is in place (can be extended in Phase 3)
- [ ] CAUT integration returns account usage data
- [ ] Workflows are audited and recoverable

## Dependencies
- Depends on Phase 1 (Foundation) - needs pattern engine, storage, watcher

## Technical Notes

### Why Codex First for Usage Limits?
- Codex has the clearest device auth flow
- Resume mechanism is well-documented
- Good test case for the complexity

### Semi-Automated Browser Auth
If Playwright can't complete auth (MFA, CAPTCHA, password):
1. Open non-headless browser
2. Request human to complete auth
3. Persist browser profile for future sessions
4. Notify when auth complete

This provides graceful degradation.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-18T08:47:54.89521276Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:07.794108825Z","closed_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:07.794108825Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of wa-nu4.1 (Phase 2: Workflows). wa-nu4.1 has correct dependency chain. Content merged conceptually."}
 {"id":"wa-cj25","title":"User pattern packs: load custom rules from ~/.config/wa/patterns/","description":"# User pattern packs

## Purpose
Allow users to define custom pattern packs that load alongside built-in packs.

## User Pack Location
```
~/.config/wa/patterns/
├── my-org-patterns/
│   ├── pack.toml        # Pack metadata
│   └── rules.toml       # Rule definitions
└── custom-agent/
    ├── pack.toml
    └── rules.toml
```

## Pack Format
```toml
# pack.toml
[pack]
name = "my-org-patterns"
version = "1.0.0"
description = "Custom patterns for internal agents"
extends = "core.codex"  # Optional: inherit from built-in

# rules.toml
[[rules]]
id = "my-org.custom_warning"
name = "Custom Warning Pattern"
pattern = "\\[INTERNAL\\] Warning: (.+)"
severity = "warning"
extract = { message = "$1" }
agent_types = ["codex", "custom-agent"]
```

## Loading Priority
1. Built-in packs (highest priority)
2. User packs in order found
3. Workspace-local packs (.wa/patterns/)

For conflicts, later packs override earlier ones unless `extends` is used.

## Implementation
```rust
pub fn load_all_packs(config: &Config) -> Result<Vec<PatternPack>> {
    let mut packs = vec![];
    
    // Load built-in
    packs.extend(load_builtin_packs()?);
    
    // Load user packs
    let user_dir = config.patterns_dir();
    if user_dir.exists() {
        for entry in fs::read_dir(user_dir)? {
            let pack = PatternPack::load(entry?.path())?;
            packs.push(pack);
        }
    }
    
    // Load workspace packs
    if let Some(workspace_dir) = config.workspace_patterns_dir() {
        // Similar loading
    }
    
    // Resolve inheritance and conflicts
    resolve_pack_inheritance(&mut packs)?;
    
    Ok(packs)
}
```

## Validation
User packs are validated on load:
- Valid TOML syntax
- Required fields present
- Regex patterns compile
- No duplicate rule IDs (within pack)

## Testing
- Unit tests for pack loading
- Tests for inheritance resolution
- Tests for conflict handling
- Tests for invalid pack rejection

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] User packs load from ~/.config/wa/patterns/
- [ ] Pack format documented
- [ ] Inheritance (extends) works
- [ ] Conflict resolution is predictable
- [ ] Invalid packs rejected with clear errors
- [ ] Tests cover all scenarios","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:54:46.618300818Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.22332-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-09T18:08:35.808039287Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-cj25","depends_on_id":"wa-ytyo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"}]}
